comprehensive_summary:
Arthur Rimbaud is a French Symbolist poet best known for 'The Drunken Boat' and the prose poem A Season in Hell. He is one of the most frequently clued poets in quizbowl, and his turbulent relationship with Paul Verlaine is a constant presence across questions.

'The Drunken Boat' ('Le Bateau ivre') is narrated by an abandoned ship that drifts 'down impassive Rivers' after its crew of haulers are killed by 'gaudy redskins' who nail them to 'colored stakes' or 'totem poles.' The boat describes striking against 'unbelievable Floridas' and seeing 'the low-hanging sun speckled with mystic horrors.' It yearns for 'Europe with its ancient parapets' and laments 'But, truly, I have wept too much! The Dawns are heartbreaking.' The boat is described as 'lighter than a cork' and 'swollen with intoxicating torpor,' and calls upon 'million golden birds, Life Force of the future.' It sees 'fish-traps where a whole Leviathan rots in the rushes' and 'singing phosphorous.' The poem was sent by the 16-year-old Rimbaud to Paul Verlaine and was heavily inspired by Jules Verne's Twenty Thousand Leagues Under the Sea. Ted Berrigan often quotes his own translations of it in The Sonnets.

A Season in Hell ('Une saison en enfer') is a prose poem that opens 'Once, if I remember correctly, my life was a banquet where every heart revealed itself.' It contains the sections 'Bad Blood,' 'The Impossible,' and two 'Delirium' sections. In 'Delirium I: Foolish Virgin,' a character addresses the narrator as 'Celestial Bridegroom' and says 'O Lord, O Celestial Bridegroom!' -- this represents Verlaine as the 'Foolish Virgin' and Rimbaud as the 'Infernal/Hellish Bridegroom.' In 'Delirium II: Alchemy of the Word,' the speaker declares 'I invented the colour of vowels!' and lists the pairings 'Black A, white E, red I, blue O, green U.' The speaker says he placed 'Beauty on my knee' and found her 'bitter.' The final 'Farewell' section declares 'One must be absolutely modern.' The poem ends with the prediction 'This is the time of the Assassins.' It was written during Rimbaud's relationship with Verlaine, after Verlaine shot Rimbaud in the wrist. Aime Cesaire's play A Season in the Congo references the title. Henry Miller wrote a study of Rimbaud titled The Time of the Assassins.

'Vowels' ('Voyelles') is a sonnet that assigns colors to the five vowels: 'A black, E white, I red, U green, O blue.' It describes A as a 'black velvety jacket of brilliant flies which buzz around cruel smells,' O as the 'Omega, violet ray of [his] eyes' and a 'supreme Clarion full of strange piercing sounds,' and features 'silences crossed by Worlds and by Angels.' It describes 'the peace of the furrows which alchemy prints on broad studious foreheads.'

Illuminations is an incomplete collection of prose poems. It opens with 'After the Flood,' in which 'a hare halted in the clover / as soon as the idea of the flood was finished' and a hare 'said its prayers to the rainbow through the spider's web.' 'Being Beauteous' describes 'whistlings of death and circles of faint music.' 'Common Nocturne' describes 'rotten rooftops.' John Ashbery published a translation of Illuminations in 2011.

The Letters of the Seer ('Lettres du Voyant') were written to Georges Izambard and Paul Demeny. They contain the famous declaration 'I is another' ('Je est un autre') and advocate a 'long, immense, and deliberate derangement of the senses' for the poet to achieve the state of seer. The letters also state 'the poet is truly the thief of fire.'

Biographically, Rimbaud gave up writing poetry at age 19-21 and spent the final years of his life gun-running in Ethiopia and East Africa, and allegedly slave trading. He was shot in the wrist by his lover Paul Verlaine. Verlaine depicted Rimbaud as an evil angel in the poem 'Crime of Love' and included him in his 1884 anthology Les Poetes maudits. Patti Smith described her 'unrequited love' for Rimbaud in Just Kids. Bruce Duffy's novel Disaster Was My God fictionalizes Rimbaud's time in Cyprus. Bob Dylan cited Rimbaud's influence, referencing Rimbaud's relationship with Verlaine in 'You're Gonna Make Me Lonesome When You Go.' Paul Valery praised Rimbaud, saying all known literature is in the language of common sense except Rimbaud's. Rimbaud was an influence on the surrealists.

works.name: A Season in Hell
works.description: A prose poem about a descent into the underworld, inspired by Rimbaud's relationship with Verlaine. Opens 'Once, if I remember correctly, my life was a banquet where every heart revealed itself.' Contains sections including 'Bad Blood,' 'The Impossible,' and two 'Delirium' sections.

clues.clue: Opens 'Once, if I remember correctly, my life was a banquet/feast where every heart revealed itself'
quotes: The line 'Once, if my memory serves me well, my life was a banquet where every heart revealed itself' opens a prose poem by him

works.name: Vowels
works.description: A sonnet that synesthetically assigns colors to the five vowels: 'A black, E white, I red, U green, O blue.' Features imagery of flies, trumpets, and angels.

works.name: Illuminations
works.description: An incomplete collection of prose poems, Rimbaud's last work. Opens with 'After the Flood' and includes sections like 'Being Beauteous,' 'Common Nocturne,' and 'Winter Feast.' Published after A Season in Hell.

works.description: Letters written to Georges Izambard and Paul Demeny, containing Rimbaud's famous declarations 'I is another' and advocating a 'long, immense, and deliberate derangement of the senses.'

works.name: Other Poems
works.description: Various individual poems including 'The Sleeper in the Valley,' 'Roman,' 'Morning of Drunkenness,' and 'Ophelia.'

clues.clue: 'The Sleeper in the Valley': an 'open-mouthed, bare-headed' soldier 'sleeping' with 'two red holes' in his side, in a 'green hole where a river sings'
quotes: a poem by this man describes an 'open-mouthed, bare-headed' man who is 'smiling as / a sick child might smile'
That sonnet by this author begins with the image of 'A green hole where a river sings' and ends with the 'two red holes'

clues.clue: 'Roman': repeats 'No one's serious when they're seventeen'; imagines heartbreak when 'the Beloved deigns to write'
quotes: a set of four interlinked poems that repeats 'No one's serious when they're seventeen'

clues.clue: 'Morning of Drunkenness': 'It began with a certain disgust'; ends with 'the time of the Assassins'

clues.clue: 'Ophelia': compared the floating Ophelia to 'a great lily'
quotes: this poet compared the floating Ophelia to 'a great lily'

works.name: General / Biographical
works.description: Rimbaud was a French Symbolist poet who gave up writing at age 19-21 and became a gunrunner in Ethiopia. He had a famous turbulent relationship with Paul Verlaine, who shot him in the wrist.

cards.front: Poem: a ship strikes against 'unbelievable Floridas' after its haulers are killed by 'gaudy redskins' who nail them to stakes
Poem: the narrator ship yearns for 'Europe with its ancient parapets' and declares 'the Dawns are heartbreaking'
Poem: the ship is described as 'lighter than a cork' and 'swollen with intoxicating torpor'
Poem: the narrator has seen 'the low-hanging sun speckled with mystic horrors' and 'fish-traps where a whole Leviathan rots in the rushes'
Poem: the narrator calls upon 'million golden birds, Life Force of the future' and sees 'singing phosphorous'
Poem: heavily inspired by Jules Verne's Twenty Thousand Leagues Under the Sea; sent to Paul Verlaine when the author was 16
Poem: a 'cold puddle' where a 'squatting child' releases a boat 'fragile as a May butterfly'
Poem: opens 'Once, if I remember correctly, my life was a banquet where every heart revealed itself'
Poem: contains two 'Delirium' sections -- 'Foolish Virgin' (representing Verlaine) and 'Alchemy of the Word'
Poem: in 'Alchemy of the Word,' the speaker declares 'I invented the colour of vowels!' and lists 'Black A, white E, red I, blue O, green U'
Poem: the speaker placed 'Beauty on my knee' and found her 'bitter'
Poem: the final 'Farewell' section declares 'One must be absolutely modern'; ends with 'This is the time of the Assassins'
Poem: contains the section 'Bad Blood'; the speaker calls himself 'a black' and recalls how his barbaric ancestors buttered their hair
Poem: assigns colors to the five vowels: 'A black, E white, I red, U green, O blue'
Poem: A is a 'black velvety jacket of brilliant flies which buzz around cruel smells'; O is the 'Omega, violet ray of [his] eyes'
Poem: O is a 'supreme Clarion full of strange piercing sounds' with 'silences crossed by Worlds and by Angels'
Work: opens with 'After the Flood,' in which a hare 'said its prayers to the rainbow through the spider's web'; includes 'Being Beauteous'
Work: John Ashbery translated this collection in 2011, claiming 'we are absolutely modern' because the author 'commanded us to be'
Work: letters to Georges Izambard and Paul Demeny declaring 'I is another' and advocating a 'long, immense, and deliberate derangement of the senses'
Work: the author states 'the poet is truly the thief of fire' in these letters
Poem: an 'open-mouthed, bare-headed' soldier 'sleeping' in a 'green hole where a river sings' is revealed to have 'two red holes' in his side
cards.back: The Sleeper in the Valley (Arthur Rimbaud)

cards.front: Author: gave up writing poetry at age 19-21 and became a gunrunner in Ethiopia and East Africa
Author: Verlaine included him in the 1884 anthology Les Poetes maudits and depicted him as an evil angel in 'Crime of Love'
Author: Henry Miller wrote a study of him titled The Time of the Assassins
Author: Patti Smith described her 'unrequited love' for this poet in Just Kids

summary: Arthur Rimbaud is a French Symbolist poet best known for 'The Drunken Boat' and the prose poem A Season in Hell.
